title: Production of Radioactive Materials
narrationScript: Every gamma source you will ever crank out of a projector started life as ordinary, stable metal. Nobody mines iridium one ninety two. We manufacture it, and in this lesson we look at how. Start with the question of stability. A nucleus is a crowd of protons and neutrons held together by the strong nuclear force, while the protons, all positively charged, push against each other. Whether that arrangement lasts forever depends on the balance between protons and neutrons. For light elements, a roughly one to one mix is comfortable. As elements get heavier, the nucleus needs extra neutrons to dilute the proton repulsion, so the stable ratio climbs toward about one and a half neutrons per proton. Plot the stable nuclides on a chart of neutron count against proton count and they form a narrow band, often called the band or line of stability. Land inside the band and the nucleus sits quietly forever. Land outside it, with too many neutrons or too few, and the nucleus holds excess energy. Sooner or later it rearranges itself and throws that energy out as radiation. That is all radioactivity is, an overloaded nucleus settling toward stability on its own fixed schedule. No furnace, no acid, no pressure changes that schedule, because chemistry only touches the electrons, never the nucleus. Now, how do we deliberately make unstable atoms? The first and most important route for radiography is neutron activation. Take a slug of stable metal, cobalt fifty nine or iridium one ninety one, and lower it into the core of a nuclear reactor where free neutrons are flying in enormous numbers. Neutrons carry no charge, so the positively charged nucleus cannot push them away. When a nucleus captures one, its mass number rises by one while the element stays the same, and the capture leaves the new nucleus with extra energy and an extra neutron that shoves it out of the stable band. Cobalt fifty nine becomes cobalt sixty. Iridium one ninety one becomes iridium one ninety two. Selenium seventy four becomes selenium seventy five. The longer the target soaks in the neutron flux, and the denser that flux, the larger the fraction of atoms converted, which is why source manufacturers quote activation times in weeks and why a hotter reactor position yields a stronger source from the same size pellet. That last point matters to image quality later. More activity packed into less metal means a smaller source, and a smaller source casts sharper shadows. The second production route is nuclear fission. Some very heavy nuclei, uranium two thirty five most famously, are so large they can split roughly in half when they absorb a neutron. Each split releases energy, two or three fresh neutrons that can go on to split more atoms in a chain reaction, and two new middle weight atoms called fission fragments or fission products. Here is the key. Heavy nuclei carry a high neutron to proton ratio, so when one splits, both fragments inherit far more neutrons than a middle weight element can hold. Fission products are therefore almost always radioactive, sitting on the neutron rich side of the stability band. Cesium one thirty seven, a gamma emitter with a half life of about thirty years, is a classic fission product, recovered by chemical separation from spent reactor fuel rather than made by activation. So keep the two recipes straight. Activation adds one neutron to a stable target you chose, and the product is the same element, one mass number heavier. Fission splits a heavy atom into two unpredictable middle weight fragments that are born radioactive. Industrial radiography leans almost entirely on activation products, cobalt sixty, iridium one ninety two, selenium seventy five, because we can pick the target, control the activity, and get the gamma energies we want. Either way, the lesson to carry forward is the same. A radioactive source is a population of atoms that we pushed out of the stable band on purpose. From the moment it leaves the reactor it is decaying back toward stability, second by second, and nothing you do in the field speeds it up, slows it down, or shuts it off. The only off switch a gamma source has is shielding and time. In the next lesson we put numbers on that decay.
imagePrompt: A technician in a glove box at an isotope production facility loads small metal pellets into a slender aluminum target capsule, with the blue glow of a research reactor pool visible through thick observation glass behind him.

sections.heading: Stable Versus Unstable Atoms
sections.body:
The nucleus is a balancing act. The **strong nuclear force** binds protons and neutrons together at extremely short range, while the protons' positive charges constantly push the nucleus apart. Whether a given combination survives depends on the **neutron-to-proton ratio (N:Z)**. Light elements are comfortable near 1:1 - carbon-12 carries 6 protons and 6 neutrons. Heavy elements need extra neutrons to dilute the proton repulsion, so the stable ratio climbs to roughly 1.5:1 - stable lead-208 packs 126 neutrons against 82 protons.

Plot every stable nuclide on a chart of neutron count versus proton count and they trace a narrow curve - the **band (line) of stability**. A nucleus inside the band is stable: left alone, it lasts forever. A nucleus outside the band - too many neutrons, too few, or simply too massive - holds **excess energy**. It is unstable, or **radioactive**, and will sooner or later rearrange itself and eject that energy as radiation.

The decay schedule is fixed by the nuclide alone. Temperature, pressure, and chemical state are electron-level business; they cannot reach the nucleus. That is why a gamma source can never be switched off - only shielded, and allowed to decay.

sections.caption: The band of stability: stable nuclides hug a curve that bends from N ≈ Z toward N ≈ 1.5Z, and nuclei pushed off the band are radioactive.

sections.heading: Neutron Activation: Building Sources to Order
sections.body:
The workhorse production method for radiographic sources is **neutron activation**. A target of stable metal is placed in the core of a nuclear reactor, where the fission chain reaction sustains an intense **neutron flux**. Because neutrons carry no charge, the nucleus cannot repel them; when a target nucleus **captures** a neutron, its mass number rises by one while its proton count - and therefore its element - stays the same. The capture leaves the nucleus with extra energy and a neutron surplus that pushes it off the band of stability:

- **Co-59 + n → Co-60** (half-life ≈ 5.27 years)
- **Ir-191 + n → Ir-192** (half-life ≈ 74 days)
- **Se-74 + n → Se-75** (half-life ≈ 120 days)

The product's activity grows with **irradiation time** and with **flux density** - a hotter core position or a longer soak converts a larger fraction of the target atoms. That is also the lever behind **specific activity** (activity per gram): more conversions packed into the same pellet means a physically smaller source for a given output, and - as you will see in the geometry lessons - a smaller source casts sharper radiographic images.

sections.caption: Neutron activation in a reactor: a stable Ir-191 target nucleus captures a free neutron and becomes radioactive Ir-192, the same element one mass number heavier.

sections.heading: Nuclear Fission: Radioactivity as a Byproduct
sections.body:
The second production route is **nuclear fission**. A few very heavy nuclides - **uranium-235** is the classic - can split into two middle-weight fragments when they absorb a neutron. Each split releases a large burst of energy plus **two or three fresh neutrons**, which can strike other U-235 nuclei and sustain a **chain reaction**. That reaction is what powers the reactor used for activation - and it also manufactures radioactive material directly.

Here is why. Heavy nuclei sit at a high neutron-to-proton ratio (around 1.5:1), but the middle-weight elements their fragments become are only stable near 1.2:1 to 1.3:1. Both **fission products** therefore inherit far more neutrons than they can hold, landing well on the neutron-rich side of the stability band. Fission products are almost always radioactive, typically decaying by beta emission followed by gamma emission.

**Cesium-137** (half-life ≈ 30 years) is the familiar example - a fission product recovered by chemical separation from spent reactor fuel. It has seen service in irradiators and some radiography, though modern industrial radiography overwhelmingly favors activation products, whose target, activity, and gamma energies can be chosen deliberately.

sections.caption: The two production routes compared.
rows: Stable target nucleus captures one neutron
Heavy nucleus (e.g., U-235) splits into two fragments
Same element, mass number +1 (chosen in advance)
Capture energy plus one extra neutron pushes it off the stable band
Fragments inherit the heavy atom's neutron excess

sections.title: Same element in, same element out - that is activation
sections.body: Neutron capture changes the **mass number, not the element**: Ir-191 in, Ir-192 out. Fission is different in kind - the original atom ceases to exist and two new, neutron-rich, radioactive elements appear. If an exam question says a stable isotope was 'placed in a reactor and became radioactive,' it is describing **neutron activation**.

sections.heading: Why This Matters in the Field
sections.body:
Production physics follows your source onto the job site.

- **It is always on.** From the moment activation stops, the source decays toward stability on its fixed half-life. There is no power switch - control comes only from shielding, distance, and time.
- **Decay sets your exposure math.** The activity printed on the source certificate is a snapshot at an assay date; every day after that, the curies fall and exposure times stretch. Lesson rt1-2-2 puts numbers on this.
- **Production quality sets image quality.** A target activated to high specific activity yields the same curies from a smaller pellet. Source physical size feeds directly into geometric unsharpness, so a well-made source is literally a sharper picture.
- **Pedigree is a legal matter.** Sealed sources are manufactured, encapsulated, leak-tested, and documented under regulatory control. The decay schedule fixed in the reactor is the same schedule your decay chart, your survey meter, and your planned exposure times must all agree with.

Know where the material comes from and the rest of the physics in this module - decay, radiation types, and energy - has a place to stand.

checkQuestions.question: What fundamentally determines whether a nucleus is stable or radioactive?
options: Its temperature and chemical state
Its neutron-to-proton balance relative to the band of stability
The number of valence electrons it carries
Whether it has ever been inside a reactor
checkQuestions.explanation: Stability is set by the neutron-to-proton ratio: nuclides inside the band of stability last forever, while those outside it hold excess energy and decay. Chemistry and temperature only affect electrons, never the nucleus.

checkQuestions.question: Iridium-191 is placed in a reactor core and emerges as iridium-192. What happened?
options: The nucleus lost a proton through fission
The nucleus captured a free neutron - neutron activation
Two iridium atoms fused into a heavier one
An electron was absorbed into the nucleus
checkQuestions.explanation: In neutron activation a stable target nucleus captures one uncharged neutron, raising the mass number by one while the element stays the same: Ir-191 + n → Ir-192.

checkQuestions.question: Why are fission products such as cesium-137 almost always radioactive?
options: They are contaminated by the uranium around them
Fission heats them above their critical temperature
The fragments inherit the heavy atom's high neutron-to-proton ratio, leaving them neutron-rich and off the stability band
They carry too many protons for their mass number
checkQuestions.explanation: Heavy nuclei like U-235 run about 1.5 neutrons per proton, but middle-weight elements are stable nearer 1.2-1.3 to 1. The fragments are born with surplus neutrons and decay, typically by beta then gamma emission.

checkQuestions.question: Which factor during production increases a source's specific activity (activity per gram), allowing a physically smaller source?
options: Longer irradiation time in a higher neutron flux
Cooling the target during irradiation
Using a chemically purer encapsulation alloy
Storing the source longer before shipment
checkQuestions.explanation: Flux density and soak time set what fraction of target atoms is activated. More conversions in the same pellet means more curies per gram - a smaller source for the same output, which improves image sharpness.

checkQuestions.question: During fission of uranium-235, what sustains the chain reaction?
options: The gamma rays released by each split
The two or three fresh neutrons released by each split, which can split further nuclei
The beta particles emitted by the fission products
Heat conducted through the fuel
checkQuestions.explanation: Each fission releases two or three neutrons; when at least one goes on to cause another fission, the reaction is self-sustaining. Those free neutrons are also exactly what activation targets soak up.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic Radiographic Physics 3.1-3.1.2 (production of radioactive materials; neutron activation; nuclear fission)
CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic Radiographic Physics 3.2 (stable versus unstable atoms)
Half-lives: Co-60 ≈ 5.27 yr, Ir-192 ≈ 74 d, Se-75 ≈ 120 d, Cs-137 ≈ 30 yr (reference values)
